professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Many cities around the world have introduced or are considering a tax on sugary drinks like sodas and energy drinks. Supporters say these taxes reduce sugar consumption and help combat rising rates of obesity and diabetes, especially among children. Critics argue that they unfairly burden lower-income households, since soft drinks are often cheaper than healthier alternatives, and that government should not micromanage personal food choices. For this week's discussion, I want to hear your view. Do you think governments should impose taxes on sugary drinks as a public health measure? Why or why not?

students.name: Diane
students.text: I'm in favor of these soda taxes, honestly. My aunt is a school nurse and she's been saying for years that the rates of childhood obesity in our town have climbed in step with the size of the soda aisle at the supermarket. When Berkeley introduced a soda tax in 2014, follow-up studies showed sugary drink consumption in low-income neighborhoods actually dropped, while purchases of water went up. The tax revenue also funded school nutrition programs. So the policy isn't just punishing people — it's redirecting money toward exactly the communities that need health support most.

students.name: Marco
students.text: Soda taxes are the wrong tool, in my view. They hit families who can't afford fancier drinks the hardest, and the actual health impact is small because people just switch to other sugary snacks. A friend of mine works at a corner store in a low-income neighborhood, and she said customers just buy candy or drive across the city line for cheaper soda. Real change comes from better school lunches, mandatory PE, and clearer nutrition labels — not from a regressive tax that treats adults like they can't make their own decisions.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Should universities require computer science students to contribute to open-source projects before graduation? Some programs, like the University of Helsinki's CS curriculum, now mandate a semester-long open-source contribution as part of the senior capstone. Advocates argue that this gives students real-world collaborative experience, exposes them to large codebases, and builds a public portfolio for job applications. Skeptics worry that beginners can overwhelm maintainers of small projects, and that not every student wants to be a software engineer — some are heading into research, theory, or game development. What's your take?

students.name: Ross
students.text: I think the requirement makes a lot of sense. When I interned last summer, my team lead told me that the single most useful thing on my resume wasn't my GPA — it was a small pull request I'd made to an open-source data visualization library. He said it showed I could read unfamiliar code, follow contribution guidelines, and handle code review. None of that is taught in a regular algorithms class. If universities want their CS graduates to actually be employable, working with real teams on real code should be the standard, not an optional extra.

students.name: Yuki
students.text: Ross makes a fair point about employability, but I disagree that this should be required. The student in the cubicle next to mine last semester spent two months trying to get a tiny doc fix merged into a popular Python library — the maintainer was overworked and unresponsive, and it nearly tanked her grade through no fault of her own. Open-source maintainers are volunteers, and forcing thousands of students to flood their issue trackers each semester would be unfair to those communities. A simulated team project inside the university would teach the same skills without the dependency on outside goodwill.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about whether restaurants should be required to phase out all single-use plastics — straws, takeout containers, utensils, and cups. Cities like San Francisco and Seattle already enforce versions of this rule, often combined with mandatory compostable alternatives. Supporters point to the massive volume of restaurant plastic ending up in oceans and landfills, while opponents argue that compostable replacements are expensive, often don't actually compost in real facilities, and pass costs to small independent restaurants. Should governments mandate the phase-out, or leave it to individual businesses?

students.name: Hailey
students.text: I think a mandatory phase-out is necessary and overdue. The voluntary approach has been tried for over a decade, and the data is clear — restaurant plastic waste keeps growing because individual businesses face a competitive disadvantage if they switch alone. A mandate levels the playing field. My city introduced one last year, and within six months the supplier market for compostables expanded dramatically and prices dropped by almost a third. Without the regulation, that market shift never would have happened. Sometimes individual choice just isn't enough to fix a collective problem.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: A mandatory phase-out sounds good in theory but ignores how compostable plastics actually behave in practice. Most municipal composters reject them because they don't break down in industrial cycles, so they end up in landfills anyway, sometimes contaminating recycling streams. Meanwhile small restaurants in my neighborhood saw food costs jump by 8% overnight after a similar mandate, and at least two closed within a year. A better approach would be investing in actual composting infrastructure and subsidizing reusable container systems before mandating bans. Otherwise we're just shifting waste around while harming small businesses.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we discussed last week, several countries — including Finland, Kenya, and parts of the United States — have piloted Universal Basic Income, giving residents a regular unconditional cash payment. The Finnish trial paid two thousand unemployed adults €560 per month for two years, and follow-up studies found modest improvements in well-being but no significant change in employment rates. Supporters see UBI as a way to provide a safety net in an age of automation, while critics question its cost and worry it may discourage work. Should governments seriously pursue UBI as national policy?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I support governments pursuing UBI seriously. The Finnish trial result is often misread — yes, employment didn't jump, but well-being and mental health improved measurably, and participants were more willing to take entrepreneurial risks. As automation eliminates more middle-skill jobs over the next decade, a basic income gives people a floor to retrain, start small businesses, or care for aging relatives without falling into poverty. The cost concern is real, but well-designed UBI replaces a lot of fragmented welfare programs and administrative overhead. It's worth piloting at a larger scale before dismissing it.

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I'm not convinced UBI is the right path. The trials so far have been too small and too short to capture what happens when an entire population receives unconditional payments. Inflation in essential goods — rent especially — would likely absorb much of the benefit, since landlords and grocers would simply raise prices. Targeted programs like expanded child tax credits, job training subsidies, and earned income tax credits show better outcomes per dollar in actual studies. Spreading limited budgets thinly across everyone, including the wealthy, isn't an efficient use of public money.
